threads.author: kira-tanaka
threads.title: March update — BYOK, multi-AI providers, and what's next
threads.body:
Hey all — time for the monthly update.

Big changes this month:

- **BYOK (Bring Your Own Key)** is live in the Console. You can plug in your own Claude, GPT-4o, or Gemini API key and use it directly from 0nmcp.com. No middle-man. Your key, your usage, your billing.
- **Multi-AI provider support** — the Console now supports Claude, OpenAI GPT-4o, and Google Gemini. Switch between them mid-conversation if you want. Each has different strengths for different tasks.
- **Membership tiers** — free tier gets you access to the community and basic tools. Pro unlocks the full Console, workflow builder, and marketplace features.

We've also been working on the **marketplace** — imagine browsing pre-built SWITCH files, one-click install, pay-per-execution. More on that soon.

What feature are you most excited about? And what's still missing that you need?

threads.author: marcus-chen
threads.title: Vault containers for team credential sharing — architecture notes
threads.body:
Spent the weekend testing the vault container system for sharing credentials across a 4-person team. Notes below.

The container format uses 7 semantic layers:

```
workflows      — .0n SWITCH files
credentials    — double-encrypted via Argon2id
env_vars       — environment variables
mcp_configs    — AI platform configs
site_profiles  — deployment targets
ai_brain       — model preferences, system prompts
audit_trail    — chain of custody
```

The escrow system supports up to 8 parties with per-layer access matrices. This means you can give your frontend dev access to the `env_vars` and `mcp_configs` layers without exposing `credentials` or `workflows`.

Actually, wait — the escrow uses X25519 ECDH for key exchange, NOT RSA. This matters because the key derivation is faster and the container files stay small.

Practical workflow:

```bash
# Creator packages everything
0nmcp vault create --layers workflows,credentials,env_vars

# Generate escrow keypairs for each team member
0nmcp vault escrow create --parties dev1,dev2,dev3

# Each team member unwraps their portion
0nmcp vault escrow unwrap --keypair dev1.keypair
```

Seal of Truth verification: `SHA3-256(transferId || timestamp || pubkey || SHA3-256(concat(ciphertexts)))`. If anyone tampers with any layer, the seal breaks.

One thing I noticed: the binary `.0nv` format starts with magic bytes `0x304E5350`. You can identify vault files at the filesystem level without attempting decryption. Nice touch for compliance scanning.

IMPORTANT: the credentials layer uses Argon2id with double encryption. Even if someone gets the container passphrase, they need the separate credentials key. Two-factor at the cryptographic level.

threads.author: priya-krishnamurthy
threads.title: CI/CD integration: running SWITCH files in GitHub Actions
threads.body:
## Overview

I have been experimenting with running 0nMCP SWITCH files directly inside GitHub Actions workflows. The goal — automated deployment pipelines that leverage the same orchestration tools we use locally.

## Setup

The approach requires three components:

1. **0nMCP installed as a dev dependency** — `npm install 0nmcp` in your project
2. **Credentials injected via GitHub Secrets** — mapped to environment variables that `0nmcp engine import` can read
3. **A wrapper script** — calls `0nmcp run` with the appropriate SWITCH file

## Example Workflow

```yaml
name: Deploy with 0nMCP
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx 0nmcp engine import --source .env.ci
        env:
          STRIPE_SECRET_KEY: ${{ secrets.STRIPE_KEY }}
          SENDGRID_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.SENDGRID_KEY }}
          SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY: ${{ secrets.SUPABASE_KEY }}
      - run: npx 0nmcp run ./workflows/deploy.0n
```

## Key Considerations

- The `engine import` step reads environment variables and creates temporary `.0n` connection files in the runner's `~/.0n/` directory. These are ephemeral — destroyed when the runner terminates.
- Rate limiting works correctly in CI — the token bucket resets per run, which is the desired behavior for deployment workflows.
- I recommend adding `npx 0nmcp engine verify` as a pre-deploy check. If any service credential is invalid, you want to fail fast — not halfway through a 12-step deployment.

## Gotcha

The vault features (`vault seal`, `vault unseal`) depend on hardware fingerprinting. This will NOT work in ephemeral CI runners because the fingerprint changes every run. Use `engine import` for CI instead — it is designed for this pattern.

Has anyone else tried this approach? I am curious whether there are edge cases I have not encountered yet.

threads.author: tiago-santos
threads.title: Replaced 3 Zapier zaps with one SWITCH file — client case study
threads.body:
So here's the story... I have this client, a small bakery chain in Porto, and they were paying EUR 49/month for Zapier Premium because they needed multi-step zaps. Three zaps running:

1. New order in Shopify → create row in Google Sheets → send confirmation email via SendGrid
2. Low inventory alert → Slack message to the owner → update Airtable tracker
3. Monthly revenue → pull from Stripe → format report → email to accountant

Each zap had 3-4 steps, and they broke constantly because Zapier's Shopify connector would lose auth every two weeks... like clockwork, you'd think they'd fix that.

I consolidated all three into a single SWITCH file with conditional branching. The trigger is a webhook — Shopify, Stripe, and a cron timer all POST to the same endpoint, and the condition step routes based on the `source` field.

The SWITCH file is 47 lines. The three Zapier zaps were... well, you can't really count lines in Zapier, but the configuration screens were maybe 60 clicks each.

Performance: the Zapier zaps had a built-in delay of 1-15 minutes (depending on plan). The SWITCH file executes in under 3 seconds. The bakery owner noticed immediately — "the confirmation emails are instant now, how did you do that?"

Cost: EUR 49/month → EUR 0 (0nMCP is free, they self-host). That's EUR 588/year saved for a small business. For my agency clients the savings are much bigger... but this one felt good because it's a real family business.

I'm thinking about writing a "Zapier to 0nMCP migration guide" — would that be useful? Like a mapping of common Zapier patterns to SWITCH file equivalents?

threads.author: elena-voronova
threads.title: The .0n standard as a data pipeline configuration format
threads.body:
As someone who spent three years writing Airflow DAGs, I want to offer a perspective on the .0n SWITCH file format that I have not seen discussed here.

The SWITCH file is not merely a workflow definition. It is a portable, validated, version-controlled unit of automation logic. In the data engineering world we would call this a "pipeline specification" and we have been searching for a good one since the Airflow `dag.py` pattern proved inadequate for anything beyond simple ETL.

## The Problem with DAGs

As Martin Kleppmann observed in Designing Data-Intensive Applications: the challenge is not executing the pipeline but expressing the pipeline in a way that is portable across environments. Airflow DAGs are Python files. They carry implicit dependencies on the Airflow runtime. Moving a DAG from development to production requires environment-specific configuration that lives outside the DAG definition.

## What .0n Gets Right

The SWITCH file bundles the what (service calls), the how (execution order, branching), and the where (connection references via `~/.0n/connections/`) into a single artifact. The variable resolution order `{{system.*}}` > `{{launch.*}}` > `{{inputs.*}}` > `{{step.output.*}}` is a clean separation of concerns that Airflow never achieved with its XCom mechanism.

The three-level execution model maps naturally to data pipeline patterns. Pipeline mode for sequential transformations. Assembly Line for parallel stage processing. Radial Burst for fan-out operations like writing to multiple sinks.

## What I Would Like to See

Schema validation on step outputs. Currently if `step.lookup.output.data[0].tier` is undefined the workflow fails at runtime. A schema declaration at the step level would catch this at parse time.

EDIT: I realize 0n-spec already has `validate()`. Perhaps the gap is documentation rather than capability.

threads.author: soo-jin-park
threads.title: The CLI output is actually beautiful?!
threads.body:
So I just ran `0nmcp engine verify` for the first time and... wait. Is this the nicest CLI output I've ever seen?

Green checkmarks for passing services. Red X for failures. Clear error messages. No wall of JSON. No "verbose mode required for details."

Why can't more tools do this? It's not hard! Color coding, clear status, actionable error messages. That's it!

I've been building CLIs in Node for 4 years and I'm taking notes from this one. The `0nmcp` command feels snappy too — cold start is under 300ms on my M2.

Questions for the community:
- Has anyone customized the CLI output format? Like piping to JSON for CI?
- Is there a `--quiet` flag for scripting?
- Any plans for a TUI (terminal UI) mode?

Also shoutout to whoever decided to use the `0nmcp` command name instead of something generic like `mcp-server`. Short, memorable, easy to type!

threads.author: arjun-mehta
threads.title: Shipped my MVP in a weekend using 0nMCP, here's the stack
threads.body:
ok so I've been talking about my AI scheduling startup for weeks and I finally shipped it, the whole thing took 48 hours from idea to production

Stack:
- Next.js frontend
- Supabase for auth + database
- 0nMCP for all the backend orchestration
- Stripe for payments
- SendGrid for transactional emails
- Google Calendar API for booking

The wild part, I wrote ZERO backend API routes. Every service interaction goes through 0nMCP SWITCH files. When a user books a slot, the SWITCH file:
1. Creates the calendar event
2. Charges their card via Stripe
3. Sends confirmation email
4. Updates Supabase with booking details
5. Notifies me on Slack

That's one SWITCH file, 5 services, runs in under 2 seconds

actually wait, I lied, I wrote ONE api route for the webhook receiver, but that's basically `0nmcp serve` with a port config

First week metrics: 847 bookings processed, 99.65% success rate, the 3 failures were Stripe timeouts during a brief outage

If you're building an MVP, stop writing glue code, just describe the workflow and let 0nMCP handle the rest

threads.author: yael-goldstein
threads.title: Security audit of the vault encryption — findings
threads.body:
Ran a security review of the vault implementation. Sharing findings.

The good:
- AES-256-GCM. Correct choice. Authenticated encryption.
- PBKDF2-SHA512 with 100K iterations. Meets OWASP 2024 recommendations.
- Hardware fingerprint binding. Credentials are machine-locked.
- Argon2id for the container credentials layer. Memory-hard. Resists GPU attacks.
- Ed25519 signatures on transfers. Correct curve for this use case.

The questionable:
- PBKDF2 iteration count is hardcoded. Should be configurable for high-security environments. Some compliance frameworks require 600K+.
- No key rotation mechanism documented. If a passphrase is compromised you need to re-seal everything manually.

The missing:
- No FIPS 140-2 validation. Expected for open source but worth noting for enterprise.
- Seal of Truth uses SHA3-256 but doesn't include a timestamp source attestation. A sophisticated attacker could replay a valid seal from a different time.

Hot take: this is more security engineering than 95% of credential managers I've audited professionally. The attack surface is small. The crypto choices are correct and modern. Most vulnerabilities would require physical access to the machine.

For anyone storing production API keys: use the vault. Do not use .env files. This is not a suggestion.

threads.author: nate-crawford
threads.title: A Rails developer's honest take on the .0n ecosystem
threads.body:
Look, I've been writing Ruby for nearly a decade, and my default reaction to any Node.js tool is somewhere between suspicion and allergic reaction (it's a defense mechanism — too many left-pad incidents). So when someone told me to try 0nMCP, I went in expecting to find the usual JavaScript ecosystem problems: dependency hell, breaking changes every minor version, configuration that requires a PhD.

I was wrong. Mostly.

The good — and I'm surprised I'm saying this — is that the architecture is clean. The service catalog is a single file. The CRM module uses a data-driven factory pattern that any Rubyist would recognize as metaprogramming done right. The tool registration is config objects, not inheritance hierarchies.

The `.0n` SWITCH file format is essentially what we'd call a "service object" in Rails, except it's declarative and portable. I can write a workflow on my MacBook and hand it to someone running Ubuntu and it just works (assuming they have the same connections configured). Try doing that with a Rails service object that depends on `ActiveRecord::Base.connection`.

What I still don't love:
- 558 tools is a LOT of surface area. In Rails we'd call this "a God object" — though to be fair, it's organized into modules that feel more like engines.
- The ESM-only approach means no `require()`. For someone who grew up on CommonJS (and before that, Ruby's `require`), this is an adjustment.
- I want a `--dry-run` flag for workflows. Let me see what WOULD happen without actually hitting APIs.

Would I recommend it to a Rails team? Yes — for the orchestration layer. Keep your Rails monolith for what it's good at (CRUD, auth, rendering) and use 0nMCP for the cross-service glue. It's the right tool for that job.

So here's my story... I run a small skincare brand on Shopify, and every morning I'd wake up and do the same things:

- Send shipping confirmation emails to customers
- Update inventory counts in Airtable

All of that... gone. One SWITCH file handles everything.

Now, I'm not going to pretend I wrote the SWITCH file myself. A developer friend helped me set it up (shoutout to anyone who helps non-technical founders like me). But once it was configured, I haven't touched it in 3 weeks and it runs perfectly every morning at 6am.

The part that honestly blew my mind... the template expressions. My friend showed me that `{{step.orders.output.count}}` pulls the actual number of overnight orders and puts it in the Slack summary message. It felt like magic.

What I want to know from the community... is there a way to trigger a workflow manually from my phone? Like, sometimes I want to send a flash sale email and I don't want to wait for the scheduled time. Is there an app or a button I can press?

Also — are there pre-built SWITCH files for common Shopify workflows? I'd love to add abandoned cart recovery but I don't want to bother my developer friend again...

threads.author: priya-krishnamurthy
threads.title: Monitoring 0nMCP workflows in production — a complete guide
threads.body:
## Problem Statement

Once you have SWITCH files running in production — especially via cron triggers or webhook receivers — you need observability. How do you know if a workflow failed at 3am? How do you track success rates over time?

## Approach 1: Built-in Logging

The `0nmcp serve` HTTP server logs every execution to stdout. In a Docker container or Vercel serverless function, these logs are captured automatically.

Key fields in the log output:
- `workflow` — the SWITCH file name
- `status` — `success` or `error`
- `duration_ms` — execution time
- `steps_completed` — how many steps ran before success or failure
- `error` — the error message if status is `error`

## Approach 2: Supabase Execution Table

Create a table to store execution results:

```sql
CREATE TABLE workflow_executions (
  id UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid() PRIMARY KEY,
  workflow_name TEXT NOT NULL,
  status TEXT NOT NULL,
  duration_ms INTEGER,
  steps_completed INTEGER,
  error TEXT,
  input JSONB,
  output JSONB,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);
```

Add a final step to every SWITCH file that logs the execution result to this table via `supabase_insert`.

## Approach 3: Slack Alerts

Add a conditional step at the end of critical workflows:

```json
{
  "name": "alert_on_failure",
  "action": "internal:condition",
  "inputs": {
    "expression": "{{system.status}}",
    "cases": {
      "error": "send_slack_alert"
    }
  }
}
```

The `send_slack_alert` step posts to your ops channel with the error details.

## Recommendation

Use all three. Logs for debugging — the Supabase table for dashboards and trend analysis — Slack for real-time alerting. The cost is one additional step per SWITCH file and a single Supabase table.

Has anyone built a monitoring dashboard on top of this pattern? I would be interested to see the Supabase queries you are using for aggregation.

threads.author: tiago-santos
threads.title: The Notion integration saved my sanity (and my client relationships)
threads.body:
Let me paint you a picture... I manage 8 freelance clients, each with their own Notion workspace for project tracking. Every Friday I'd spend 2 hours copying status updates from each Notion board into a summary email for the client. It was like being a human middleware, you know?

So I wrote a SWITCH file. It queries all 8 Notion workspaces in parallel (Assembly Line mode, obrigado 0nMCP), pulls the status of every active project, formats them into client-specific email templates, and sends via SendGrid.

The parallel execution is key here... querying 8 Notion APIs sequentially would take maybe 12-15 seconds because Notion's API is, well... let's just say it takes its time like a Portuguese lunch. With Assembly Line mode it's under 3 seconds because all 8 queries fire simultaneously.

The template expressions are doing heavy lifting:

```
{{step.notion_client_1.output.results | filter(status='In Progress') | count}}
```

That gives me the number of in-progress tasks for client 1. Then I can say "You have {{count}} tasks in progress this week" in the email. Clean.

Funny thing... one client replied to the automated email saying "I love how organized your updates are now, they used to be all over the place." Little do they know it's a robot now doing what I was doing badly as a human 😄

Anyone else automating Notion? I feel like there's a gold mine of patterns we could share...

threads.author: yael-goldstein
threads.title: PSA: rotate your API keys. Now. Here's how with 0nMCP.
threads.body:
If you haven't rotated your API keys in the last 90 days, you're overdue.

Here's the fastest path:

```bash
# 1. Generate new keys in each service dashboard
# 2. Update your .env or connection files
# 3. Re-import
0nmcp engine import --source .env
# 4. Verify everything still works
0nmcp engine verify
# 5. Revoke the old keys in each service dashboard
```

Do step 5 LAST. Not first. Not in parallel. Last. I have seen production outages from teams who revoked before verifying the new keys worked.

If you're using the vault:

```bash
# Re-seal with new credentials
0nmcp vault seal --force
```

The `--force` flag overwrites the existing sealed vault. The old vault data is gone. Keep a backup of the old vault file if you need rollback capability.

Hot take: most "security incidents" with API integrations are not sophisticated attacks. They're leaked keys in git commits, shared .env files on Slack, and keys that haven't been rotated since the Mesozoic era. The vault exists to solve all three problems. Use it.

One more thing. If you're storing API keys in environment variables on Vercel or similar: those are encrypted at rest by the platform. But they're decrypted on every cold start and exist in plaintext in the function's memory. The vault's hardware binding means a stolen vault file is useless without the specific machine. Different threat model.

threads.author: marcus-chen
threads.title: Stripe webhook verification — how it actually works in 0nMCP
threads.body:
Seeing questions about webhook security. Here is how the verification works under the hood.

0nMCP uses HMAC-SHA256 for Stripe webhooks. The flow:

```
1. Stripe sends POST with Stripe-Signature header
2. Header format: t=timestamp,v1=signature
3. 0nMCP constructs signed payload: `${timestamp}.${rawBody}`
4. Computes HMAC-SHA256(signed_payload, webhook_secret)
5. Compares computed signature with v1 from header
6. Rejects if timestamp is >5 minutes old (replay protection)
```

The relevant code is in `webhooks.js`. It supports 6 providers:
- Stripe (signature header)
- CRM (X-Hook-Secret header)
- GitHub (X-Hub-Signature-256 header)
- Twilio (X-Twilio-Signature header)
- Shopify (X-Shopify-Hmac-Sha256 header)
- Generic HMAC (configurable header name)

IMPORTANT: do NOT use `express.json()` middleware on webhook routes. It consumes the raw body and the signature verification will fail. Use `express.raw()` or read the raw buffer.

```javascript
// Wrong
app.use(express.json())
app.post('/webhooks/stripe', handler) // signature fails

// Correct
app.post('/webhooks/stripe', express.raw({type: 'application/json'}), handler)
```

This is documented but I see the mistake in every third Stripe integration I review. The raw body bytes MUST match what Stripe signed. JSON parsing and re-serializing changes whitespace.

threads.author: olivia-pearce
threads.title: Non-technical founder's guide to understanding SWITCH files
threads.body:
TL;DR: SWITCH files are recipes for your computer. Here's how to read them without knowing how to code.

So I've been using 0nMCP for about a month now, and I kept seeing these `.0n` files everywhere but they looked intimidating with all the curly braces and technical terms. Until I realized... they're just recipes.

Think of it like cooking:
- The **name** is the recipe title ("chocolate-cake")
- The **trigger** is what starts the recipe (the oven timer going off, or in our case, a webhook or scheduled time)
- The **steps** are the instructions ("mix flour", "add eggs", "bake at 350")
- The **inputs** are the ingredients you bring ("customer email", "order amount")
- The **{{template expressions}}** are the measurements ("2 cups" becomes "the email from step 1")

Here's a real example translated into plain English:

```
Name: new-order-notification
When: A new Shopify order comes in
Do:
  1. Get the customer's email from the order
  2. Look up their name in our Supabase database
  3. Send them a personalized thank-you email via SendGrid
  4. Post a message to our team Slack channel
  5. Add a row to our Google Sheet for tracking
```

That's it. That's a SWITCH file. The curly braces and JSON formatting are just the language the computer needs it in... but the LOGIC is exactly as simple as what I wrote above.

I hope this helps other non-technical founders feel less intimidated. The community here has been incredibly patient with my questions...

What other concepts would you like me to explain in plain English?

threads.author: nate-crawford
threads.title: Testing SWITCH files: the missing piece
threads.body:
So here's my (admittedly opinionated) take on the one thing the 0nMCP ecosystem needs more than anything else: a testing framework for SWITCH files.

In Rails, we have RSpec. In Node, you have Jest (or Vitest, or whatever this week's testing framework is — I can't keep up). But for SWITCH files? You run them against real APIs and pray.

What I want:

1. **Mock mode** — Run a SWITCH file where every service call returns a configurable mock response. No API calls. No network. No rate limiting.
2. **Snapshot testing** — Record a workflow's API calls once, save the responses, replay them on subsequent test runs.
3. **Step isolation** — Test individual steps without running the entire workflow. Like unit testing for automation.
4. **Assertions** — "After step 3, the output should contain a `customer_id` field." Basic, but essential.

The `--dry-run` flag I've been asking about would be step zero of this. Show me the plan without executing it.

As DHH says (yes, I'm quoting the Rails creator in a Node forum, fight me): "The goal of testing is confidence." Right now, the only way to be confident a SWITCH file works is to run it against production APIs. That's fine for simple workflows. For the 34-step client onboarding Jake described? You want a safety net.

Is anyone else feeling this, or am I bringing Rails baggage to a different paradigm?

threads.author: kira-tanaka
threads.title: Tips for writing great forum posts (and getting helpful replies)
threads.body:
I've noticed some patterns in which posts get great replies and which ones get crickets — so here are some tips from your friendly community lead.

**Posts that get great responses:**
- Include your setup (what services, what version of 0nMCP, what OS)
- Share what you've already tried
- Paste the actual error message (not "it doesn't work")
- Keep code snippets focused — don't paste your entire SWITCH file if the problem is in step 3
- Use the right group — **help** for questions, **showcase** for projects, **tutorials** for how-tos

**Posts that struggle:**
- "How do I automate?" without any context about what you want to automate
- Screenshots of code instead of text (can't search or copy from images)
- Multiple unrelated questions in one post

No judgment if your past posts fell into the second category — we all learn these things over time. The community is here to help either way.

Also — if someone helps you, hit that upvote. It means a lot and it surfaces good answers for future searchers.

What other community guidelines would you find helpful?

threads.author: jake-holloway
threads.title: Cold email automation: what's possible with 0nMCP + Smartlead
threads.body:
Just connected Smartlead to our 0nMCP stack. Sharing the setup.

1. Lead list lives in Supabase. Enriched with company data.
2. SWITCH file pulls leads matching criteria (industry, company size, role).
3. Sends to Smartlead via API for sequenced cold email delivery.
4. Smartlead handles the actual sending (warm-up, rotation, deliverability).
5. Replies come back through webhook → CRM contact creation → pipeline assignment.

Why not just use Smartlead alone? Because the lead qualification BEFORE sending matters. The SWITCH file filters, scores, and enriches. Smartlead just delivers.

Results after 2 weeks:
- 847 emails sent across 3 campaigns
- 23% open rate
- 4.7% reply rate
- 12 qualified meetings booked

Cost: $0 for 0nMCP orchestration + $39/month Smartlead.

The ROI on those 12 meetings paid for a year of Smartlead in week one.

Hot take: cold email isn't dead. Bad cold email is dead. When you personalize with real data (pulled from multiple sources via 0nMCP), the response rates speak for themselves.

threads.author: elena-voronova
threads.title: ETL patterns with SWITCH files — replacing Airflow for small teams
threads.body:
I have been asked several times in this forum whether 0nMCP can replace a traditional ETL framework. The answer is nuanced and depends on scale.

## When SWITCH Files Replace Airflow

For teams processing fewer than 100K records per day across fewer than 20 data sources the overhead of maintaining an Airflow instance (scheduler, webserver, metadata database, workers) is not justified. A SWITCH file running via `0nmcp serve` with a cron trigger achieves the same outcome with approximately 2% of the operational complexity.

As Kent Beck reminds us: "Make it work, make it right, make it fast." Most teams never need to get past "make it work" for their data pipelines.

## Pattern: Extract-Transform-Load with SWITCH

```json
{
  "name": "daily-etl",
  "trigger": "cron:0 6 * * *",
  "execution": "pipeline",
  "steps": [
    {"name": "extract", "action": "mongodb_query", "inputs": {"collection": "events", "filter": {"date": "{{system.yesterday}}"}}},
    {"name": "transform", "action": "internal:map", "inputs": {"source": "{{step.extract.output}}", "expression": "flatten_and_dedupe"}},
    {"name": "load", "action": "supabase_upsert", "inputs": {"table": "analytics", "data": "{{step.transform.output}}"}}
  ]
}
```

## When to NOT Replace Airflow

- More than 1M records per batch (memory constraints)
- Complex DAG dependencies with retry semantics per task
- Regulatory requirements for pipeline audit trails
- Teams larger than 10 data engineers who need the Airflow webserver UI for coordination

## The Middle Ground

Use SWITCH files for the orchestration layer and Airflow for the heavy computation. The SWITCH file triggers the Airflow DAG via API and monitors its status. Best of both worlds.

EDIT: I should mention that the `internal:transform` action in 0n-spec supports basic data transformations including flatten, filter, group, and aggregate. Sufficient for most small-to-medium ETL workloads.

threads.author: kira-tanaka

Quick announcement — the **off-topic** group is officially open for conversations that don't fit neatly into the other categories.

Want to share a cool article about AI? Off-topic. Having a debate about tabs vs spaces? Off-topic. Found a hilarious programming meme? You guessed it — off-topic.

The only rule: be kind and keep it SFW (safe for work). Everything else is fair game.

I know some of you have been posting tangential discussions in general and that's totally fine, but now there's a dedicated space for it. The general group will stay focused on 0nMCP-related announcements and discussions.

Also — I've been thinking about starting a weekly "What are you building?" thread. Would Mondays or Fridays work better for most people?

threads.author: priya-krishnamurthy
threads.title: Rate limiting deep dive — how 0nMCP prevents API throttling
threads.body:
## Background

One of the most underappreciated features of 0nMCP is its built-in rate limiting. I have examined the implementation in `ratelimit.js` and want to share my understanding for those running high-throughput workflows.

## Architecture

The rate limiter uses a **token bucket algorithm** with per-service configuration. Each service in the catalog defines:

- `rate_limit` — requests per second
- `burst` — maximum tokens in the bucket (allows short bursts above the sustained rate)
- `backoff_factor` — multiplier for exponential backoff on 429 responses

When a tool invocation is requested:
1. The limiter checks if a token is available for that service
2. If yes — the token is consumed and the request proceeds
3. If no — the request is queued and retried after the bucket refills
4. On a 429 response — the limiter activates exponential backoff: `delay = base_delay * backoff_factor^attempt`

## Practical Implications

### Stripe
Stripe allows 100 requests per second in live mode. The 0nMCP catalog sets `rate_limit: 25` — a conservative 4x safety margin. This means even under Radial Burst execution you will not hit Stripe's limits.

### SendGrid
SendGrid's rate limit varies by plan but is typically 100 requests per second for the API. The catalog sets `rate_limit: 10` — generous headroom.

### CRM
The CRM API has aggressive rate limiting — approximately 10 requests per second with hard throttling. The catalog reflects this at `rate_limit: 5`.

## Custom Overrides

If your use case requires higher throughput — for example, a bulk migration — you can override rate limits in your `.0n` connection file:

```json
{
  "service": "stripe",
  "rate_limit": 50,
  "burst": 75
}
```

Be cautious with overrides. The defaults exist because they have been tested against real API responses.

Has anyone encountered rate limiting issues in production? I am curious about edge cases where the defaults proved insufficient.

threads.author: tiago-santos
threads.title: My client wants to switch from Make.com — migration checklist
threads.body:
Got a message from a client yesterday... "Tiago, Make.com raised their prices again. What are my options?" Classic.

So I'm building a migration checklist for moving from Make.com (formerly Integromat) to 0nMCP SWITCH files. Sharing my draft here for feedback.

**Step 1: Audit existing scenarios**
Make.com exports scenarios as JSON blueprints. Download every active scenario. Count the services used, the number of steps, and any custom logic (filters, iterators, aggregators).

**Step 2: Map services**
Make.com has "modules" — 0nMCP has "tools". The mapping is usually 1:1 but the naming differs. For example Make's "HTTP" module = 0nMCP's generic REST tool, Make's "Iterator" = 0nMCP's `internal:map`.

**Step 3: Recreate as SWITCH files**
Each Make.com scenario becomes one SWITCH file. The branching logic that Make handles visually with "routers" becomes `internal:condition` steps.

**Step 4: Test in parallel**
Run both systems for a week. Compare outputs. This is critical — don't cut over without a validation period.

**Step 5: Cut over**
Disable Make.com scenarios. Update webhooks to point to `0nmcp serve` endpoints.

The tricky parts... Make.com has a visual builder that non-technical users love. 0nMCP's workflow builder on 0nmcp.com helps but it's not as polished yet. For clients who manage their own automations, this is a real consideration.

Also, Make.com's scheduler is built in. With 0nMCP you need external cron (Vercel cron, GitHub Actions, crontab). Not hard but it's a step.

Anyone else migrated clients from Make.com? What did I miss?

threads.author: yael-goldstein
threads.title: Container escrow: sharing credentials without sharing credentials
threads.body:
The vault container escrow system solves a problem I've been thinking about for years: how do you give a team member access to specific API keys without giving them ALL the keys?

0nMCP's approach: per-layer access matrices with multi-party key exchange.

Scenario: 3-person team.
- Lead dev needs everything
- Frontend dev needs env_vars and mcp_configs only
- Contractor needs workflows only

```bash
# Create container with all layers
0nmcp vault create --layers all --passphrase $PASS

# Generate escrow with per-party access
0nmcp vault escrow create \
  --parties lead:all,frontend:env_vars+mcp_configs,contractor:workflows
```

Each party gets a `.keypair` file. X25519 ECDH derives a shared secret per party. The container encrypts each layer separately. Each party can only decrypt the layers they were granted.

The lead dev can verify who has access to what:

```bash
0nmcp vault inspect container.0nv
# Shows: 3 parties, per-layer access matrix, seal integrity
```

If the contractor leaves: `0nmcp vault revoke --party contractor`. Re-seal. The old keypair is useless.

This is real zero-trust credential management. Not "put it in 1Password and share the vault." Cryptographic enforcement of access boundaries.

The Seal of Truth ties it together. Any modification to any layer invalidates the seal. You can verify at any time that the container hasn't been tampered with since creation.

threads.author: nate-crawford
threads.title: 558 tools is a feature, not a bug
threads.body:
I wrote a post a while back questioning whether 558 tools was too many (the "God object" concern). I've since changed my mind, and I think it's worth explaining why.

The analogy that helped me was Unix. `ls`, `grep`, `awk`, `sed`, `cat`, `sort`, `uniq` — there are hundreds of Unix commands. Nobody complains that Unix has too many tools. Why? Because each one does one thing, and they compose via pipes.

0nMCP is the same pattern. Each tool does one thing (create a Stripe payment intent, send a SendGrid email, query Supabase). SWITCH files are the pipes. The MCP protocol is the shell.

The question isn't "are 558 tools too many?" — it's "can I find the one I need?" And the answer is yes, because:

1. Tools are organized into 26 services. Want Stripe? Look in the Stripe service. Done.
2. The Claude integration means you can say "list tools that create Stripe subscriptions" and it filters for you.
3. The catalog is a single file (`catalog.js`). You can literally `grep` for what you need.

Compare this to the alternatives:
- Zapier: 6,000+ apps. Nobody calls that too many.
- Make.com: 1,700+ modules. Same.
- The difference: those are closed platforms. 0nMCP is a single npm package.

I was wrong about the God object concern. It's a tool belt — and a big tool belt is better than a small one if the tools are organized well. (DHH would probably disagree — but then again, Rails has 200+ ActiveRecord methods on every model, so...)

threads.author: soo-jin-park
threads.title: The workflow builder UX — what works and what could be better

So I've been playing with the visual workflow builder on 0nmcp.com and I have THOUGHTS!

**What works great:**

**What could be improved:**
- No undo! If I delete a step by accident, I have to rebuild it. This is the #1 thing.
- The template expression helper needs more examples. Like, how do I reference step outputs? I have to check the docs every time.
- Mobile responsiveness. I tried building a workflow on my iPad and the drag-drop didn't work.
- Dark mode. Please. My eyes.

**What I dream about:**
- Version history for workflows. Git-style diffs between versions.
- Collaboration. Multiple people editing the same workflow. Like Figma for automation.
- A "test this step" button that runs just that one step with sample data.

Hmm, actually the "test this step" idea is basically what Nate was asking for with his testing framework request. Maybe that's the same feature?

Who else has builder feedback? Tagging @kira-tanaka since she mentioned the team reads feature requests!

threads.author: arjun-mehta
threads.title: Supabase + Stripe + 0nMCP = the whole backend for my SaaS
threads.body:
I keep telling people this but nobody believes me, my entire SaaS backend is three things: Supabase for the database, Stripe for payments, 0nMCP for everything else

no Express server, no custom API routes, no Lambda functions, nothing

here's how it works:
- Supabase handles auth, database, and real-time subscriptions
- Stripe handles billing, subscriptions, invoices
- 0nMCP connects them and handles all the business logic via SWITCH files

when a user signs up:
1. Supabase auth creates the user
2. Database trigger fires a webhook
3. 0nMCP SWITCH file catches it
4. Creates Stripe customer
5. Sends welcome email via SendGrid
6. Adds to CRM
7. Posts to our team Slack

actually wait, I do have ONE Next.js API route, the webhook endpoint. but that's literally just forwarding to 0nmcp serve

monthly costs:
- Supabase free tier: $0
- Stripe: 2.9% + $0.30 per transaction
- 0nMCP: $0 (open source)
- SendGrid free tier: $0
- Vercel free tier: $0

total fixed cost: $0/month. I'm profitable from transaction one

EDIT: someone asked about the Supabase database trigger → webhook part. you set it up in Supabase dashboard under Database → Webhooks. point it at your 0nmcp serve endpoint. it fires on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE

replies.author: marcus-chen
replies.body:
The multi-AI provider support is a good move. For anyone wondering about the practical difference:

- Claude (Sonnet/Opus): best for code generation and complex reasoning
- GPT-4o: strongest at following structured output formats
- Gemini: largest context window, good for analyzing long documents

BYOK is the right architecture. Platform-managed API keys create a single point of failure and a billing bottleneck. Letting users bring their own keys means the platform scales without proportional API costs. This is how production tools should work.

replies.author: olivia-pearce
replies.body:

replies.author: kira-tanaka
replies.body:
Love the enthusiasm, Adaeze! To answer your question — yes, you CAN switch between AI providers mid-conversation. The model picker in the Console is live. Pick Claude for one message, switch to GPT-4o for the next.

The key thing to understand — each provider has its own pricing. Claude charges per token through your Anthropic account. GPT-4o charges through your OpenAI account. Gemini through Google. So if you're budget-conscious, you might want to use a cheaper model for simple questions and save the powerful ones for complex tasks.

Also, your API keys are stored in your browser's local storage only. They're never sent to our servers. The Console makes direct API calls from your browser to the AI provider. Zero middleman.

replies.author: nate-crawford
replies.body:
The `engine verify` output is good — I'll give it that. Clean, readable, no unnecessary noise. It's like the developers actually use their own CLI (a radical concept in the JavaScript ecosystem, I know).

To answer your questions:
- `--json` flag exists for CI piping. Outputs structured JSON instead of the pretty format.
- `--quiet` suppresses everything except errors. Good for scripts.
- TUI mode — I actually asked about this too. Would love a `htop`-style view showing active workflows, execution counts, and service health.

replies.author: marcus-chen
replies.body:
Solid audit. Agree on all points.

Regarding the PBKDF2 iteration count — you can work around this by using vault containers instead of the basic vault for high-security environments. The container credentials layer uses Argon2id which is configurable and memory-hard. The basic vault's PBKDF2 is fine for 99% of use cases.

On key rotation: I built a SWITCH file that automates rotation for Stripe and SendGrid. Generate new key → update .0n connection → verify → revoke old key. Works for any service with an API key rotation endpoint. Happy to share if anyone wants it.

The timestamp attestation point is valid. A signed timestamp from a trusted source (like a TSA) would strengthen the Seal. That said, the replay window is narrow because the transferId includes a random component. Practical exploitation would require both the vault file AND the ability to forge or replay within the same transfer context.

replies.author: soo-jin-park
replies.body:
Haha Nate, the "too many left-pad incidents" line got me! But seriously, your take on the .0n format as a portable service object is spot on.

The ESM thing though — I actually love it? No more `module.exports` vs `export default` confusion. One way to do imports. Clean.

And YES to `--dry-run`! That's the feature I didn't know I needed until you said it. Show me the execution plan without hitting APIs. Like `terraform plan` but for automation. That would make the builder SO much more useful for iteration!

replies.author: marcus-chen
replies.body:
Good question. The three levels map to concurrency patterns:

```
Pipeline:       step1 → step2 → step3
                (sequential, each waits for previous)

Assembly Line:  [batch1: step1,step2] → [batch2: step3,step4] → [batch3: step5]
                (parallel within batch, sequential between batches)

Radial Burst:   step1 ─┬→ step2
                       ├→ step3
                       ├→ step4
                       └→ step5
                (one trigger, everything fires simultaneously)
```

Assembly Line is for when you have GROUPS of independent operations that need to complete before the next group starts. Example: query 5 databases in parallel (batch 1), then process all results together (batch 2).

Radial Burst is for fan-out with NO dependencies between steps. Example: a new user signs up, and you simultaneously create accounts on 5 services.

Yes, you can mix them. The `execution` field can be set per-step group, not just per-workflow.

replies.author: tiago-santos
replies.body:
Adaeze, think of it like cooking a big meal...

**Pipeline** = making pasta. You boil water, THEN cook the pasta, THEN drain it, THEN add sauce. Each step needs the previous step done.

**Assembly Line** = a restaurant kitchen. Station 1 preps all the salads at once. When ALL salads are done, station 2 plates them all at once. Parallel within each station, sequential between stations.

**Radial Burst** = a potluck dinner. Everyone starts cooking their dish at the same time. Nobody waits for anyone else. Everything comes together at the end.

The key question to ask yourself: "does this step need the output of a previous step?" If yes → Pipeline. If groups of steps are independent but groups depend on each other → Assembly Line. If everything is independent → Radial Burst.

And yes, you can mix! Use Pipeline for the first 3 steps, then Radial Burst for steps 4-7. You specify it in the SWITCH file's execution config.

replies.author: jake-holloway

Yes. Manually triggering workflows from your phone.

Option 1: Bookmark the webhook URL. Open it in your phone browser. The SWITCH file triggers.

Option 3: Slack shortcut. Send a Slack message like "/trigger flash-sale" and have a Slack bot forward it to your webhook.

For pre-built Shopify workflows — the marketplace is coming. That's exactly what it's for. One-click install, pay once, run forever.

In the meantime, check the tutorials group. Several of us have shared Shopify SWITCH files you can adapt.

replies.author: elena-voronova
replies.body:
The AI-in-the-loop pattern you describe is indeed supported and it opens a design space that traditional workflow engines cannot access.

However I would caution against placing LLM calls in the critical path of latency-sensitive workflows. The 800ms you measured is typical for a small prompt. Complex classification tasks with few-shot examples can take 2-4 seconds. Multiply by the number of tickets per minute and you may find the queue growing faster than the processor.

The pattern I prefer: batch classification. Accumulate tickets for 5 minutes. Send them to the LLM in a single request with all tickets as context. Parse the structured response. Then route all tickets simultaneously via Radial Burst.

This amortizes the LLM latency across many items and reduces API costs by 60-80% compared to per-ticket classification.

EDIT: The Anthropic `anthropic` service in the 0nMCP catalog supports `batch` mode. Worth investigating.

replies.author: arjun-mehta
replies.body:
the Supabase execution table idea is great, we're basically doing the same thing

our version has one extra column: `cost_cents`, we calculate the approximate API cost of each execution based on which services were called. helps us understand unit economics per workflow

for the Slack alerting, we added a severity level too:
- info = workflow completed successfully
- warn = workflow completed but with retries
- error = workflow failed

only `warn` and `error` go to Slack. otherwise the channel becomes noise and people ignore it

actually wait, there's a fourth case: `timeout`. some workflows run longer than expected because of slow third-party APIs. we alert on those separately because they often indicate an upstream issue, not a bug in our workflow

replies.author: yael-goldstein
replies.body:
To answer your specific question about whether --dry-run is different from a testing framework:

Dry-run = show me what WOULD happen. Read-only inspection.
Testing = show me what DID happen and verify it matches expectations.

Both are needed. Dry-run is defensive. Testing is assertive.

The mock mode Nate described would work. Implementation sketch:

```bash
0nmcp run workflow.0n --mock --mock-dir ./fixtures/
```

Each service gets a directory of response fixtures. The runner loads them instead of making real API calls. Step outputs are computed from fixtures. Assertions check the computed outputs.

This is basically what we do for security testing of API integrations. Record real responses once, redact sensitive data, replay forever. Deterministic, fast, free.

replies.author: priya-krishnamurthy
replies.body:
## Additional Webhook Verification Details

Good writeup, Marcus. I want to add context for the GitHub webhook verification specifically, as it has a subtlety.

GitHub uses `X-Hub-Signature-256` which is HMAC-SHA256 of the **entire raw body**. However — GitHub also sends `X-Hub-Signature` (without the 256) which uses HMAC-SHA1. The 0nMCP implementation correctly prioritizes the SHA-256 header.

For anyone setting up GitHub webhooks:

1. Generate a strong secret: `openssl rand -hex 32`
2. Set it in your GitHub webhook configuration AND in your `.0n` connection file
3. The `0nmcp serve` webhook handler validates automatically — no additional code required

One edge case I encountered — if you are behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Cloudflare), ensure the proxy passes the raw body through. Some proxy configurations re-encode the body, which breaks HMAC verification. The symptom is a consistent 403 on every webhook delivery.

replies.author: kira-tanaka
replies.body:
Soo-jin, this is great feedback — passing it directly to the team.

Undo is on the list. You're not the first person to accidentally delete a step and have to rebuild it (I've done it myself during a demo, which was fun).

The "test this step" button is actually being discussed right now as part of a broader debugging improvement. It would essentially be: select a step, provide sample input data, click "test", see the output. No need to run the whole workflow.

Dark mode — it's coming. The CSS already supports it via `prefers-color-scheme` for most of the site, but the builder canvas needs specific attention because of the node colors and edge styles.

Keep the feedback coming — this is exactly how the product gets better.

replies.author: tiago-santos
replies.body:
Arjun, this is basically what I'm telling all my clients to do... the Supabase + Stripe + 0nMCP stack is the indie hacker's dream.

One thing I'd add to your setup — backup. You have Supabase for the database but are you backing it up? The free tier doesn't include point-in-time recovery. I learned this the hard way when a client accidentally deleted a table and we had... nothing.

Set up a nightly SWITCH file that exports your Supabase tables to a Google Sheet or an S3 bucket. 10 minutes of work now saves you from catastrophic data loss later.

Also... $0/month total cost is incredible but don't forget to factor in your time. The cheapest tools are expensive if they take 10 hours to configure. The nice thing about 0nMCP is the configuration time is genuinely low — your 48-hour MVP story proves it.

Muito bom, parabéns on the launch!

replies.author: marcus-chen
replies.body:
The Smartlead integration is solid. One optimization:

Instead of pulling leads and sending to Smartlead in separate steps, use Assembly Line mode to parallelize the enrichment. If you're enriching 100 leads sequentially, that's 100 API calls * ~200ms each = 20 seconds. Assembly Line with batches of 10 brings it under 3 seconds.

```json
{
  "execution": "assembly_line",
  "batch_size": 10
}
```

Also — for the reply webhook from Smartlead, make sure you're verifying the webhook signature. Smartlead uses HMAC-SHA256. Add the signing secret to your .0n connection file and the `0nmcp serve` handler validates automatically.

Re: open rates — 23% is strong for cold email. Are you warming the sending domains through Smartlead or separately? Domain warm-up makes a significant difference in deliverability.

replies.author: yael-goldstein
replies.body:
GitHub webhook → Discord notification is a good starter project.

Two security notes:
1. Set up a webhook secret in GitHub. Without it, anyone who knows your endpoint URL can send fake push events.
2. The `0nmcp serve` handler validates GitHub signatures automatically if you have the secret in your connection file.

The SWITCH file is ~5 lines: receive webhook, extract repo name + commit message, format Discord embed, POST to Discord webhook URL.

Don't forget to filter events. GitHub sends webhooks for pushes, PRs, issues, comments, stars, forks — everything. Your SWITCH file should check `{{inputs.event_type}} == 'push'` before triggering the Discord notification. Otherwise your channel gets flooded.

also the morning routine automation is SO relatable, I used to spend like 30 minutes every morning just checking things and copying data between apps. now I just check Slack for the summary. FREEDOM!!

replies.author: yael-goldstein
replies.body:
The per-layer access matrix is the right approach. Most credential sharing failures happen because of all-or-nothing access.

One concern: the X25519 keypair files. Where do team members store them? If a keypair file is on a laptop that gets stolen, the attacker can unwrap their layers.

Recommendation: encrypt the keypair file at rest with a passphrase. The vault's own PBKDF2 can do this. Double encryption: Argon2id on the layer contents + PBKDF2 on the keypair itself.

Also: log escrow events to the audit_trail layer. Who unwrapped what, when. If a credential leak is detected, you need the forensic trail to identify the source.

The `vault inspect` output should include last-access timestamps per party if possible. Current implementation only shows creation-time metadata.
